company.name: DoorDash
company.description: DoorDash operates a local commerce platform connecting consumers, merchants, and delivery workers across restaurants, grocery, retail, and related services.

overview.title: Business model
paragraphs: DoorDash runs marketplaces that connect consumers, merchants, and delivery workers, earning revenue from marketplace commissions, consumer fees, advertising, and membership programs such as DashPass, Wolt+, and Deliveroo Plus.

Its 2025 annual report describes a much larger global footprint after the Deliveroo acquisition, with over 56 million monthly active users and more than 35 million members across its subscription programs at year end.

overview.title: Scale and economics
paragraphs: DoorDash reported 3.2 billion total orders, $102.0 billion of Marketplace GOV, and $13.7 billion of revenue for 2025, showing that the company has moved beyond a narrow restaurant-delivery app into broader local commerce infrastructure.

Profitability has improved, with 2025 GAAP net income attributable to common stockholders of $935 million and adjusted EBITDA of $2.8 billion, but the business still depends on dense local networks, logistics execution, merchant participation, and consumer retention.

moatNarrative: DoorDash's moat is primarily a local network-density moat: more consumers attract more merchants, more order volume improves delivery-worker utilization, and better selection plus faster fulfillment reinforces consumer habit. The company also benefits from brand recognition, app placement, merchant tooling, advertising surfaces, and subscription-driven retention.

The moat is not purely technological. It is operational and regulatory as much as software-based, which makes it durable in dense markets but exposed to labor classification rules, fee caps, merchant dissatisfaction, and local cooperative alternatives that can win on trust or economics in narrower geographies.
decentralizationNarrative: DoorDash is structurally centralizing because pricing, ranking, dispatch, consumer identity, membership economics, dispute handling, and merchant visibility are mediated by one corporate platform. That creates a clear target for cooperative, federated, and peer-to-peer marketplace models.

Decentralization is credible at the neighborhood or city level before it is credible globally. Open food marketplaces, courier cooperatives, federated local-commerce protocols, and transparent reputation layers can replicate parts of DoorDash's function, but matching its liquidity, real-time logistics, fraud controls, insurance, support, and consumer convenience remains difficult.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 151-175; metrics refreshed on 2026-05-29 using 2025 DoorDash filings and current market-data pages.

moat.rationale: DoorDash has strong marketplace liquidity, consumer habit, merchant reach, advertising inventory, and subscription retention, reinforced by 3.2 billion 2025 orders and over 56 million monthly active users. The score is capped because local commerce remains city-by-city, labor-intensive, and vulnerable to regulation and merchant alternatives.

decentralizability.rationale: Ordering, merchant discovery, delivery dispatch, reputation, and settlement can be decomposed into local or federated systems, and projects such as CoopCycle and Open Food Network show viable pieces. Real-time fulfillment density, support, fraud control, and consumer habit make full displacement harder.

profitability.rationale: DoorDash reported 2025 GAAP net income attributable to common stockholders of $935 million and adjusted EBITDA of $2.8 billion, a material improvement from earlier loss years. Profitability is still moderate relative to marketplace GOV and exposed to delivery costs, regulation, and growth investments.

peRatio.rationale: StockAnalysis reported a trailing P/E ratio of 75.92 for DASH near the May 2026 review date, reflecting a high growth multiple after the company's return to GAAP profitability.

marketCap.rationale: StockAnalysis reported DoorDash market capitalization of approximately $69.40 billion near the May 2026 review date.

products.name: DoorDash Marketplace
products.summary: DoorDash Marketplace lets consumers order from restaurants, grocers, retailers, and other local merchants for delivery or pickup through DoorDash's app and website.
products.whyItMatters: The marketplace is DoorDash's core demand aggregator and logistics coordination layer, concentrating consumer traffic, merchant discovery, pricing, delivery dispatch, and support inside one platform.
replacementSketch: A credible replacement would not start as a universal DoorDash clone. It would begin with a city, neighborhood, university, cooperative grocery network, restaurant association, or courier collective that already has local trust and repeat demand.
Open-source storefronts, cooperative delivery software, transparent fee schedules, and interoperable reputation could reduce platform rent while preserving enough convenience for repeat use.

alternatives.name: CoopCycle
alternatives.summary: CoopCycle is a federation and software platform for worker-owned bike delivery cooperatives, offering logistics and ecommerce tools for local delivery groups.

openness.rationale: CoopCycle publishes software and describes it as open access with a reciprocal license, but access is intentionally tied to cooperative governance rather than permissive unrestricted commercial reuse.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The federation model supports autonomous local courier cooperatives rather than one centralized platform owner, making it a strong decentralization fit for city-scale delivery.

readiness.rationale: CoopCycle has real operating cooperatives and a complete platform, but its footprint and category breadth are much smaller than DoorDash's consumer marketplace.

costLeverage.rationale: Cooperative ownership and shared software can reduce extractive platform fees, but local operators still carry labor, insurance, dispatch, and customer-support costs.

alternatives.name: Open Food Network
alternatives.summary: Open Food Network is an open-source marketplace platform for farmers, food producers, distributors, and community food hubs.

openness.rationale: Open Food Network explicitly describes itself as open-source software, and the project maintains a public repository.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The platform is designed for local producers, hubs, and community food enterprises rather than centralized restaurant delivery, making it strong for local-food commerce but less complete for instant courier dispatch.

readiness.rationale: Open Food Network reports use by more than 2,500 enterprises in over 15 countries, but it is oriented toward local food ordering and hubs rather than DoorDash-style on-demand delivery.

costLeverage.rationale: Open-source marketplace software can lower entry costs for community food sellers and hubs that would otherwise rely on commercial marketplace tooling.

disruptionConcepts.name: Federated Local Delivery Cooperatives
disruptionConcepts.summary: A federation of city-level courier cooperatives could replace parts of DoorDash Marketplace by letting restaurants and local retailers join a locally governed delivery network using shared open software, transparent fees, and interoperable ordering surfaces.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept changes the market from one national platform extracting fees from many local markets into many locally owned delivery networks that share software, standards, and mutual support while keeping pricing and governance local.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through cooperative ownership, federated software, and local governance rather than Bitcoin. Restaurants, couriers, and communities can coordinate without routing every rule change, fee, and ranking decision through one corporate intermediary.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Restaurants publish menus and delivery zones through local cooperative instances; courier-members accept shifts or jobs; consumers order through local storefronts; federation-level standards handle shared tooling, onboarding practices, and inter-coop learning.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Order fulfillment is verified through merchant confirmation, courier pickup and drop-off events, customer receipt confirmation, and cooperative dispute processes. Local membership and repeated interaction reduce anonymous fraud, while shared software logs constrain false completion claims.
failureModes: Local co-ops may fail to reach enough consumer demand or merchant selection to match incumbent convenience.
Governance can become slow or fragmented, especially when pricing, dispatch priority, and worker standards conflict.
Fraud and service-quality controls may lag centralized platforms unless federation tooling is strong.
adoptionPath: Start in dense neighborhoods where restaurants already object to platform fees and bike delivery is operationally realistic.
Bundle ordering, dispatch, payment, and merchant support into a cooperative service with transparent local pricing.
Federate successful city networks through shared software, brand trust, and common dispute and data standards.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The mechanism directly shifts ownership and control from a central platform to local cooperative operators connected by federation.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: CoopCycle demonstrates that delivery cooperatives can coordinate through shared software, but scaling liquidity and reliability across many markets remains difficult.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The software and cooperative pattern already exist, but market-by-market operations, insurance, merchant sales, customer acquisition, and support are execution-heavy.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The concept can pressure DoorDash in fee-sensitive local niches but is unlikely to displace national marketplace liquidity quickly.

disruptionConcepts.name: Lightning-Native Local Commerce Settlement
disruptionConcepts.summary: A local-commerce marketplace could use open ordering software with instant low-fee settlement between consumers, merchants, and couriers, reducing reliance on platform-custodied payment flows and making smaller direct transactions more practical.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept attacks the payment and settlement layer of platform control by letting merchants and couriers receive transparent, near-real-time payments while marketplace operators compete on discovery and logistics rather than custody and opaque fee bundling.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Lightning is central as the settlement rail for small, frequent local transactions, tips, courier payouts, refunds, and escrow-like payment flows. Bitcoin matters less as a speculative asset than as open payment infrastructure that no marketplace operator exclusively controls.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Consumers place orders through a local marketplace; merchants accept priced invoices; couriers accept delivery tasks with route and payout terms; settlement is split among merchant, courier, and network operator according to visible rules.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Payment release can be staged around merchant acceptance, courier pickup, and customer delivery confirmation. Fraud resistance depends on signed order events, reputation, dispute windows, and local operator accountability; it does not eliminate the need for human support.
failureModes: Consumer willingness to use Lightning for everyday food and retail ordering may remain too low.
Disputes, substitutions, refunds, chargebacks, and tax handling are harder with irreversible payment rails.
Regulatory and accounting burdens could outweigh payment-cost savings for mainstream merchants.
adoptionPath: Begin with opt-in merchant and courier payouts while consumers continue using conventional payments.
Add Lightning discounts, instant courier tips, and transparent fee splits in communities already familiar with Bitcoin payments.
Expand toward full order settlement only where refunds, taxes, and customer-support workflows are proven.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Open payment settlement can reduce platform custody and improve direct merchant-courier economics, but discovery and dispute handling can still recentralize.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The payment mechanics are plausible, but mainstream consumer behavior, refund handling, and merchant accounting remain significant barriers.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: A pilot can be built on existing marketplace software, but production-quality settlement, compliance, and support workflows are nontrivial.

incumbentPressure.rationale: This would pressure DoorDash only in narrow communities unless consumers and merchants value open settlement enough to change ordering habits.

products.name: DashPass
products.summary: DashPass is DoorDash's consumer membership program offering delivery-fee and service-fee benefits on eligible orders.
products.whyItMatters: DashPass strengthens consumer retention and order frequency, turning DoorDash from an occasional convenience app into a recurring local-commerce subscription.
replacementSketch: A lightweight replacement would bundle benefits across locally governed food, grocery, and courier networks rather than locking the membership to one corporate marketplace.
The strongest version would make membership fees visible and allocable: part to software maintenance, part to courier guarantees, part to merchant promotion, and part to local support.

alternatives.name: CoopCycle
alternatives.summary: CoopCycle can support locally governed delivery networks whose recurring member or supporter models substitute for a centralized delivery subscription.

openness.rationale: The platform is open access under reciprocal cooperative terms, with public code and a federation model.

decentralizationFit.rationale: A recurring-benefit model tied to local delivery co-ops would keep member value and governance local rather than centralized under DoorDash.

readiness.rationale: CoopCycle is operational for cooperative delivery, but a DashPass-like multi-merchant consumer membership would need local packaging and consumer marketing.

costLeverage.rationale: Local membership fees could fund shared infrastructure and fairer courier economics, though they may not match DoorDash's scale discounts or promotional budget.

disruptionConcepts.name: Portable Local Commerce Membership
disruptionConcepts.summary: A portable local-commerce membership could let consumers subscribe once to a neighborhood or city network of merchants, couriers, and food hubs, receiving transparent benefits across participating services without locking loyalty to one national app.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept changes subscriptions from a centralized retention tool into shared local infrastructure funding. Membership value becomes portable across cooperative delivery, local food hubs, and merchant-owned storefronts.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through federated identity, portable membership credentials, and cooperative governance. Bitcoin is not necessary unless the network later adds open settlement or micropayment-based benefit accounting.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Members join a local network; merchants and courier co-ops publish benefit rules; membership revenue is allocated by transparent formulas based on orders, participation, and governance decisions; federation standards let the model interoperate across cities.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Membership eligibility can be verified through signed credentials or account attestations. Benefit abuse is constrained through merchant-side redemption logs, order records, rate limits, and local dispute processes.
failureModes: Consumers may prefer the simplicity and selection of DashPass over a smaller local membership.
Allocating subscription revenue among merchants, couriers, and software maintainers can create governance conflict.
Portable credentials do not solve delivery reliability or support quality by themselves.
adoptionPath: Launch with a small set of restaurants, groceries, and courier co-ops that already share customers.
Offer simple recurring benefits such as reduced delivery fees, priority windows, or member-only local promotions.
Standardize portable membership credentials and revenue-sharing rules as more local networks join.

decentralizationFit.rationale: A portable membership shifts recurring consumer lock-in away from one app and toward locally governed networks.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The model can coordinate through existing local marketplace and co-op tooling, but revenue sharing and benefit standardization are hard.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: A basic membership layer is feasible, but making it portable, trusted, and economically meaningful across multiple local operators requires governance and software work.

incumbentPressure.rationale: This can weaken DashPass in values-aligned or merchant-led communities, but DoorDash's scale, selection, and app habit remain major defenses.

summaryMarkdown: Created a new DoorDash registry bundle with two products: DoorDash Marketplace and DashPass. The refresh uses DoorDash 2025 filing and investor sources for scale and profitability, market-data sources for valuation metrics, and CoopCycle/Open Food Network as credible open or cooperative alternatives.
